answer:
Event $A$ contains the sample points $H T$ and $T H$; thus, $A$ is a compound event. Because two or more sample points cannot occur at the same time, we can easily calculate the probability of event $A$ by summing the probabilities of the two sample points. Thus, the probability of observing exactly one head (event $A$ ), denoted by the symbol $P(A)$, is

$$
P(A)=P(	ext { Observe } H T)+P(	ext { Observe } T H)= rac{2}{9}+ rac{2}{9}= rac{4}{9}
$$


Similarly, because $B$ contains the sample points $H H, H T$, and $T H$,

$$
P(B)= rac{4}{9}+ rac{2}{9}+ rac{2}{9}= rac{8}{9}
$$

meta_info.caption: The Probability of a Compound Event-Coin-Tossing Experiment
